preamble: The close front rounded vowel [y] can undergo a phonetic transformation to the open-mid front unrounded vowel [ɛ] through a process of unrounding and lowering. This shift is notably attested in the historical development of English, particularly within the Kentish dialect during the Middle English period.
phoneticEffects: This transformation involves two primary phonetic changes: unrounding, where the lips lose their rounded posture, and lowering, where the tongue position moves from a close (high) to an open-mid position in the mouth

examples.note: The long close front rounded vowel in Old English, as in 'mȳs' (mice), was unrounded and lowered to a long open-mid front unrounded vowel in Kentish Middle English.
The short close front rounded vowel in Old English, as in 'hyll' (hill), was unrounded and lowered to a short open-mid front unrounded vowel in the South-East (Kentish) Middle English.

sources: Martín Díaz, M. A. (2020). A three-fold development of Old English y in Middle Kentish place-names. Estudios Ingleses de la Universidad Complutense, 10, 137-155.
